body:
MEDIUM, erasure-integrity. Found by the loam-erasure lens during T67's P5 (2026-07-23).

THE BUG. `QuarantinePool.drop()` removes the pool from `gw.quarantinePools` and closes it. Its interface comment says drop 'detaches the pool from the primary's erasure fan-out, closes it, and DISCARDS ITS STORE WHOLESALE'. That is true only for the default MemoryBackend. With `QuarantineOptions.backend` set to a sqlite or archive store — the documented way to run a long-running quarantine — drop closes the handle and leaves the seeded copies ON DISK.

After that, every `erase` walks `gw.quarantinePools` and so never reaches that file, and T67's new `holds` verdict is asked only of `gw.backend`. A record erased afterward survives inside the operator's own walls, in a store nothing will ever sweep again — precisely the erasure-evasion channel §24.8's fan-out exists to prevent. And `erase` reports a clean completion over it.

The comment OVERCLAIMS what drop delivers, which is its own defect: a reader trusting it would believe dropping is safe.

THE FIX (Myk's call on which):
  (a) drop() purges the pool's store of everything it was seeded with before closing — 'discards wholesale' made true; or
  (b) drop() REFUSES for a caller-supplied durable backend, forcing an explicit `drop({ purge: true })` or a documented hand-off; or
  (c) the primary records dropped-pool locators and future erasures sweep them too — most complete, most machinery.
Recommend (a) for a pool the gateway seeded, since the pool's whole contents are by construction a replica the primary can reconstruct.

RAILS: drop a pool backed by a real ArchiveBackend, then erase a record the pool held, and assert at the FILESYSTEM that the vault holds no bytes of it. Object level, not API level — the pool object is gone by then, so an API assertion cannot see this class at all.


--- DECIDED (Myk, 2026-07-24, in chat): (a) WITH (b) ---
drop() PURGES a durable pool's bytes before close — discard means discard, at every backend; a container is a staging area, never a hiding place (§28.2). PLUS a detach()/reattach pair: detach closes the handle and deliberately KEEPS the store (the operator takes responsibility, the act is named), and a detached pool can later be REATTACHED once ruled safe — 'a temporary quarantine,' in Myk's words, for debugging a suspect pool without destroying evidence. Design note for the build: a DETACHED durable store is bytes outside the erasure fan-out and outside health's pool walk — the detach record should land as a claim (who detached what, where it lives) so /health or repair can at least NAME the detached stores as unproven surface rather than forgetting them entirely. Rails must be two-sided: drop leaves NO bytes (holds/heldAmong on a reopened handle of the same path) AND detach leaves ALL bytes plus the record; reattach restores erasure reach (an erase after reattach sweeps it).

--- BUILT (branch t72-drop-detach, stacked on design-container-arc) ---
drop purges+verifies over everything the pool can NAME (readable ∪ session reactor ∪ the §25 pen), refusing attached on survivors or an unanswerable probe; detach is the deliberate keep; openQuarantine SETTLES erasure debt against the primary's surviving tombstones before the pool reader boots, refusing stores it cannot prove clean — which closes the detach-window hole for every open, not just reattach. Verifier CONFIRMED the settle (timing, forgiveness-skip, fault model). RESIDUALS, named not papered: (1) a concurrent gw.erase() completing MID-openQuarantine lands a tombstone in neither the settle snapshot nor the fan-out (the pool is not yet attached) — an interleaving outside the sequential window, single-operator usage makes it marginal; the closing move if it ever bites is re-checking readTombstones after quarantinePools.add. (2) drop's jurisdiction is what a read or this session NAMED — a straggler bearing an unlisted id is heal's domain (stated in the interface). (3) mirror-only tier divergence pre-heal is enumeration-blind (pre-existing mirror doctrine). (4) admit-predicate reseeds carry no negation closure (pre-existing, T38-adjacent) — newly exercised by reattach flows.
title: QuarantinePool.drop() leaves a durable pool's bytes on disk, outside every future erasure's reach
